In order to prevent the spread of the Ebola virus into Liberia, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has recommended that there be a quarantine of members of the local population or those who produce or trade in the region. The epidemic has infected more than 10,000 people in the last two weeks, and a further 4,000 in the first three weeks of this week.

"Over the last 24 hours, we have heard from about 5,000 people in the proximity of the Ebola outbreak, and we also heard from the Sierra Leone government, who have sent out what they call 'guy-in-the-middle' protection precautions," said Dr. Catherine Daniele, director of the CDC's Inspection and Control Center.

The fact that most of the people who died in that region were in Liberia is a significant problem, she added.

"This has been a huge problem in the past week, and everyone is talking about an Ebola outbreak in Liberia," she said. "There are still the rumors about who is responsible. Somebody in the security forces, who are working with them, are saying they're not sure who is responsible, who is responsible for the Ebola outbreak or whether there's a lone-wolf — or we are not sure."

Dr. Daniele said the current threat to the region is likely to be stronger and possibly more intense by the end of the week.

"At the same time, as we were talking about the people who died in the region, there is also another factor that is putting this outbreak into focus: There is an epidemic of people who are hosting their own Ebola symptoms, that is voracious of the primate virus," she said. "We need to make sure that people are treated and if they are not treated, they are not going to get to the hospital."

The launch of the first round of quarantine measures was announced this week by the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services as part of the CDC's HealthDay Summit.

The CDC's three-day conference focused on infectious disease control in local communities and government health systems.

Zachary Garrison, a senior infectious disease specialist at the CDC, was one of the speakers.

Garrison said three of the three Ebola outbreaks he's been to in recent years have involved people who had been exposed to the virus in the United States. He said his office's priority is to speak to the people who are affected and to educate them about what they need to do to minimize the spread of the disease.

"Because of the health risks we have, our primary focus is to do outreach, to make sure our activity is providing quality care that is effective, and to make sure our community partners are taking the appropriate steps to protect their own health and safety," he said.

Francesco Cirillo, a journalist with The Travel Channel, said the outbreak moved quickly in Liberia and has affected people all over the world.

"Now, there are people out in Africa and Central America who are sick. We have to be very careful about the health risks that are going to be transported in, and it's going to be a very difficult and dangerous journey," he said.

Cirillo said he has heard from people in the region who know who the person is.

"They're not talking to HealthDay. They're not talking to anybody. Who knows who they are, but they're people who are sick and are living with Ebola," he said. "And their health is very, very difficult. It's a very, very, very difficult journey that we're on."

A man who was arrested while fleeing the scene on Tuesday morning and then later charged with aggravated battery on a police officer was still in custody.

On Tuesday morning, an Austin Police Department officer who responded to a marijuana occurrence at a home around 8:30 a.m. reported seeing a man in a wheelchair with a black shirt and a scarf out.

The officer responded, and when he decided to follow the man out of the house, he was confronted by a man who was wearing a black hooded sweatshirt and a hat.

The man, who is in his 40s, was arrested on suspicion of assault with a deadly weapon, and was put in custody for a short time. He was released on a $100 bond and is expected to appear in court on Feb. 9.

The Austin Police Department is investigating the incident and will release more information at a later date.

Last year, a University of Queensland researcher reported that a clear black mark on the brain of healthy participants had been found to be caused by toxic genes.

Jeremy Dalrymple, a cognitive psychologist at the University of Queensland, said the findings were particularly troubling given the large differences in the brain's behaviour between individuals who received the same treatment and those who did not.

"In fact, the variability in brain chemistry between humans speaks directly to the variability of genes," Dr Dalrymple told ABC News Australia.

He suggested the findings in the latest study could help scientists understand how different brains have different functions.

The findings also highlight the need to develop better treatments that target specific chemicals in the brain before being linked to other disorders, Dr Dalrymple said.

"These are fundamental questions, so I think if we can use these findings to help clinicians understand the biology of the brain, then there might be ways that we can work around the behaviour of people," Dr Dalrymple said.

The researchers have not yet reviewed the data from a previous study in which they had examined 21 male participants.

About 2 per cent of participants said they had a positive reaction to a drug to treat a particular illness. About 20 per cent of participants said they had a negative reaction to drugs to treat a particular disease.

Dr Dalrymple said many people with atypical responses to drug therapy, such as those who have been treated unfairly in the past, were not as susceptible to the disorder as the general population.

He said these people were not often the same person as they were not at risk.

"If you're not at risk, and it's clear that you don't have an exact number of people that are at risk for the disorder, it's very, very difficult for the general population to be able to make those adjustments in the same way," Dr Dalrymple said.

The report from the University of Queensland, published in the journal Psychosomatic Medicine, was funded by NIH Grant MH0230133, the Australian National Institutes of Health's National Institute of Mental Health, and the National Institutes of Health.

Topics: brain, health, diseases-and-disorders, mental-health, research, science-and-technology, psychology-research, australia

Syracuse, N.Y. (AP) — A Syracuse school board member and several of his top aides with ties to the Trump campaign are facing federal charges for secretly organizing meetings to solicit campaign contributions from the president's son, Donald Jr.

At least one of the former state senator's aides, Eric Seabrook, pleaded guilty this week to conspiring to knowingly organize the meetings.

The charges, unsealed Monday, accuse Seabrook and his deputy and his son, Anthony, of funneling $2 million in political funds to a pro-Trump super PAC in exchange for dirt on Trump.

Seabrook and his son Sean have pleaded not guilty.

The charges in federal court against Seabrook and his son allege that they conspired to help Trump get elected.

The indictment, unsealed Tuesday, alleges that Seabrook and his son "acted with intent to influence the outcome of the 2016 presidential election as part of their campaign to recruit and elect pro-Trump officials who would act as intermediaries for the president's son."

Prosecutors say that the Trump campaign hired Seabrook, and later hired Sean Hannity, a former Fox News show host, to write a book about the tapes and "determine whether the tapes and Hannity's book would be released."

Seabrook and his son, Sean, were indicted on two felony counts each for conspiring to wire money in a joint presidential campaign, conspiracy to violate sanctions and conspiring to cause a crime.

Several other members of the state House, including Seabrook and Sean Hannity, have been indicted on charges related to the tapes.

The alleged scheme is part of the ongoing investigation into the Trump campaign's alleged coordination with Russia's campaign and its ties to the Russian government.

Seabrook was one of eight members of the Republican majority who have pleaded guilty to federal charges stemming from the group's alleged links to Russia's range of activities.

The charges allege that Seabrook and his son helped the Trump campaign create a "false flag" operation to look into the presidential race, which is what prosecutors say began in early 2015.

Using fake documents, the Trump campaign was able to point to the president's son as a source of information about the group's efforts to influence the election, according to the indictment.

Lawyer Robert Gilberry, representing Seabrook, said Friday that he was surprised by the indictment, which comes at a time when the Trump campaign is exploring a possible prosecution.

"This is really a very serious matter. This is a very serious matter," Gilberry said.

The indictment alleges that Seabrook and his son "made more than $3 million in contributions to the Trump campaign prior to providing personal services" to the Trump campaign.

In the indictment, the indictment says Seabrook and his son "created a false flag operation" to hide the Trump campaign's efforts to influence the presidential race, which is what prosecutors say began in early 2015. The probe also alleged that Seabrook and his son responded to a State Department email sent by Trump's son, then-candidate Donald Trump Jr., about finding out the contents of the email.

Prosecutors accused Seabrook and his son of "retaliation for falsely promising to 'evolve' the group," to resist pressure to reveal the email. In an analysis published Thursday, the Washington Post found that the documents had been made public under pressure.

Prosecutors say the emails show that Seabrook and his sons began arranging meetings with the Trump campaign in the middle of the 2016 campaign with a "hundreds of people who were represented on the Clinton campaign staff," as well as "a variety of campaign staff and lawyers."

The emails appeared to indicate that the Trump campaign had a plan to obtain information about the meeting.

After the meeting, the emails show that Seabrook and his son turned over the email, which the prosecutor said had said it was an "e-mail from a third party" about which they would share more information.

Prosecutors say that Seabrook and his son thanked Hannity for his help with the meeting, which Hannity had called "the most concrete thing I've ever seen in my life."

Prosecutors say that after Hannity called it a "bombshell" and that the campaign "rolled up a massive flag" before taking the meeting, the emails appeared to backtrack.

Seabrook and his son pleaded not guilty in federal court in Syracuse on Monday.

The indictment, unsealed Monday, also alleges that Seabrook and his son "expressed confidence in the integrity of the campaign's discussions with the campaign because they believed it would be a positive contribution to the effort."

Hannity and Hannity have also been charged with engaging in campaign related activities.

The Gulf Coast Palm Beach County Sheriff's Office said the suspect was identified as James A. Johnson, 37, of Pima Beach, Fla. He was taken into custody by a Palm Beach County Sheriff's Office helicopter and booked onto a flight to Nome, California.

The suspect was taken into custody at 10:30 a.m. early Sunday morning heading to Nome, according to the Palm Beach County Sheriff's Office.

Detectives said a flight at Nome, which is operated by the Nome Regional Airport, took off from the airport as Johnson was being transported there and took off with the suspect on board. Deputies said Johnson got into an altercation with the suspect and was shot once in the back.

The Sheriff's Office is investigating.

According to the Sheriff's Office, the suspect was also booked into the Nome Regional Airport.

Johnson was arraigned Tuesday morning at Palm Beach County Jail on two counts of aggravated assault with a deadly weapon. He was released on $1,000 bail.

The challenge

The city's Central and South China Railway line is the world's longest and most costly railway. It travels from China's capital Beijing to the capital then to the mainland on either side of the border with Taiwan.

The Chinese government says its plan to build the project, called 'The Great Wall of China', will save it $45 billion.

In 2012, the city completed a feasibility study on the project, but has been unable to successfully complete it in time for the opening of the Hong Kong National Exhibition in December.

China's government continues to build rail networks across the country, including the Beijing Central Railway (CCR) and Guangzhou Railway (HSR).

"The Great Wall of China is the longest building project in China," said Hong Kong's Railway Institute

"The government has successfully pushed the project forward and has done exploration and evaluation, but the work is still not completed," Hong Kong's Time reported.

The project's 10-metre-high wall is the world's tallest.

The project is due to open in mid-2015 but its location is disputed by China's central government.

The last time the project was completed, in June, the 10-metre high China Wall was raised to create a 600-metre-wide high-speed line to bring the city's economic hub to a close.

A year later, construction company Zhongtong Construction Group, owned by Chinese billionaire Hu Jintao, is planning to build a 1.7-metre high-speed rail line to the city.

In advance of the 18th Annual National Basketball League Awards, the NBA announced that its 2017-18 member Magic Johnson will have to take some time off from basketball.

Johnson refused to participate in the NBA All-Star Game, which was held last night in Toronto, due to "personal reasons" and "personal reasons and personal reasons."

"I'm so sorry for that decision," Johnson said in an exclusive interview with ESPN. "It was a personal decision. I tried to have fun, but I am very sorry for what happened last night."

Johnson received a standing ovation from the crowd when he spoke about the decision.

In addition to his 50th NBA season, Johnson is the NBA's first NBA All-Star since 1989. The former Magic Johnson will be the first player in NBA history to win an All-Star Game (along with Jimmy Butler, Shaquille O'Neal, Russell Westbrook, Carmelo Anthony and Kevin Love) as they will represent the league in a triple-double tiebreaker. During the contest, Johnson led a parade through the Garden of Eden for the National Basketball Association's Most Valuable Player.

Johnson will miss his final game for the Magic on Feb. 1 following a knee injury, but he will get regular-season training in early March.

"I'm excited to play in front of the fans and to be able to play for them, and I'm looking forward to this season," Johnson said in a statement. "With all due respect to coach Magic, I am very happy to be the official champion of the NBA. I've done my very best to be the best I can be, and I'm excited to try my best to continue to get better. I'm also excited to be able to work with my teammates to be the best I can be."

The NBA announced that the NBA Players Association will host a press conference on Feb. 1, 2018, to discuss the NBA Players Association's decision to not honor its All-Star Game. The league also announced it will be hosting a public holiday on Feb. 23, 2018, in honor of the 15th anniversary of the game.

NBA All-Star center and MVP Paul George will join the Hall of Famers on Feb. 26, 2018. George is the NBA's 14th All-Star.

Hundreds of demonstrators gathered in front of the headquarters of the Democratic National Committee Thursday evening in Chicago after a day of protests over the decision to remove DNC chairwoman Debbie Wasserman Schultz.

Demonstrators held signs reading "Lifepoint Debbie Wasserman Schultz" and the "We the People" sign," according to the Associated Press.

Organizers of the "We the People" protest said they are holding a "very important event, focusing on issues of workers' rights and economic justice."

"This is the first time in the history of the Democratic Party that we've had a major political party nominated by a member of the party who stood up against corruption in the DNC. I think that's important," said Phil LaPorte, one of the organizers of the rally.

But Rep. Debbie Wasserman Schultz, a Democrat, appeared to be a reluctant defender of free trade agreements.

The party's decision to remove her from office had been met with protests among black, Latino and other ethnic groups.

In a statement issued Wednesday, the DNC said it would "take all steps to build a Democratic Party that is strong, inclusive, and confident and supports workers' rights."

"This is about the future of our country as a whole and the future of our democracy as a whole," the statement read.

The Democratic National Committee was also forced to remove Wasserman Schultz after a tape surfaced online of her remarks about African-American labor, which was later released by WikiLeaks.

The Democratic statement said that the DNC "will maintain an open dialogue with all parties to address the issues facing our country, as well as with members of other parties, including the president and Democrats across the country."

A new study by the Department of Health & Human Services, released Monday, details the country's aging population, and the challenges the government faces in reaching its target of doubling the country's lifeline.

The study, entitled "Conservation of Lifeline and Prospects for Lead from Fuel Cell Components in 2050," was commissioned by the Health Care and Development Authority and was released to the press by the USDA in response to Cecilia DeCesare's Center for Health Policy.

SPONSORED

The study found that while the number of U.S. residents grown to 65 has declined by 12 percentage points in the past decade, the number of children growing up in poverty has continued to grow.

"The U.S. population's aging is certainly doing much better than most of the world's," said David Buitenweger, director of the USDA's Center for Health Policy. "But the United States remains a global leader in the development of the world's most cost-effective and beloved every-day lifeline system, which has contributed to the reduction in population in the United States."

The study, conducted by researchers from the U.S. country's Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, found that children with chronic conditions – neurological, psychiatric, and developmental illness – are at much greater risk of developing lead contamination than children with developed diseases.

In a separate study, researchers also found that the more children are exposed to lead, the more these children have a higher risk of developing lead exposure.

Lead from fuel cells is the most abundant component in gasoline, and its use is increasing, according to the FDA. Part of the reason for the tremendous increase of lead exposure is the loss of natural resources and the resulting higher risks for those in the water or in the air.

Lead from fuel cell components is also a major source of phthalates, which are used if the chemistry of the fuel cell is not properly understood. These phthalates are also found in some metals.

The study was published in the December 17, 2016 issue of The Lancet, a British medical journal.

"The primary concern in developing countries in the developing world is the increasing use of lead in fuel cells," said DeCesare. "The question is whether this will be addressed. If it is, what will it do to the economy and how will we plan to make it free of lead?"

DeCesare is working with researchers from the T.I.H. Department of Health Care and Development Authority, the U.S. National Institute of Child Health and Human Development, the National Institute on Aging, the National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences, the U.S. Geological Survey and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency to conduct a national analysis of lead from fuel cell components and lead from fuel cell components from 4,024 water wells, based on the findings of the study's preliminary analysis.

The research was funded by the USDA and is supported by the MMWR Washington Office on Emerging Infectious Diseases.

— One of the oldest public libraries in Rockville, Maryland, has been criticized by conservative groups for failing to ensure the free use of its public records.

The library's three-story, 25-room building, for example, has been under fire for not providing new information based on eligibility requirements.

This fall, the Justice Department complained that the library's free information policies were inadequate, and that the library's policy on access to documents is to review documents, not provide new information.

The government responded by asking that there be a public hearing about the policy, which has not been made public.

"We're hopeful that we're going to finally get the documents that we need to provide them to the public the way we need them to be," said Laurel Dallman, director of the library's Office of Information Technology and Information Technology Services.

The city of Rockville (3497 N. Harrison St.) countered that the library is aware that its users are required to reform their government.

"This document is free, but it's also open access to what is available," said Linda Cennett, director of the Department of Public Information.

She said the library's policy on data is to review documents every two years.

There is no available data on how many copies to save, she added, but officials said they are aware of about 150,000 rolls of copies every year.

Dallman said there are no legal cases involving the library, and the government has no involvement in the legal proceedings.

The library's staff said they was in touch with the city and have "every reason to believe that they have the right to provide the data that the library is required by the rights of the person acquiring it," said Michelle Bautista, manager of Rockville's public information department.

The library's response from the city is that the information it collects is exempt from disclosure.

"The library does not have all the information that it needs to provide the public about the program," Bautista said.

The library's legal team did not immediately return a request for comment.

In a statement, the city said the city's policy has been "intended to provide the public with essential information that is free from disclosure, but is not free from government agencies."

"The library's policy in this case is not applicable to the overall system of access to and access to information provided by the federal government," the city said.

The New York State Library's Freedom of Information Act protects public records the state government possesses.

The privacy-insurance initiative, which was co-sponsored by the state and federal government, created the Library Information Rights Act of 1974, which makes it legal for the state and the federal government to collect data about individual persons.

The legislation was written to protect privacy and information about the lives of many people, but it is most recently amended to allow more control over individual privacy rights, including access to protected documents and data.

The law has been criticized by conservative groups for not providing clear written language about how the state may collect information of people involved in the Internet.

"It says that the state may only access information that the public has been given the right to know, and it is intended to protect that right," said Darla Walker, a lawyer with the Center for Freedom of Information and Democracy.

The state has frequently pushed back against the law, and it has said the law is more responsive to the privacy interests of information-seeking businesses.

"The open access of documents is a critical component of the state of the nation's approach to the Internet," said Illinois Secretary of State Kris Kobach in a statement to The Daily Caller News Foundation. "We did not propose or guarantee freedom of the press or the public from the First Amendment of the U.S. Constitution."

Both organizations have questioned the constitutionality of the law.

"It is simply a whole lot of quibbling within the bounds of the First Amendment," said Reisner. "It is still relevant to the public's right to know, and it is a matter of good faith, but it is clear that the government is able to collect and maintain records at a reasonable level of quality for a wide variety of purposes."

The National Library of Medicine, which has a policy that limits the government's ability to collect information about patients, said it is disappointed the government has not responded to its concerns about open access to the library's records.

"This is a good example of why we want a complete system of records protection," said Alison Smith, director of library services for the National Library of Medicine. She said the policy helps ensure the future of library services

One morning, after taking a shower, the three youngsters took shelter in a tree near their home in Pune. The animals were brought to the vet, who told them to go home with their mates, with GHB still on their hands. The three children were then taken to a brothel, where they were given injection injections of GHB. They were given two half sheets a day, and further injections were given all day.

"Just a couple of weeks ago, when they were being injected, they had an idea that they were going to be taken to another brothel. But they weren't," said a senior medical officer (CNO). "When they came back into the place, they found none of the GHB, and the others had been injected by the diazepam. They began to believe they were going to be taken to a brothel where they could get better healthcare and the treatment of these animals."

After injecting them with the "real" GHB, the children were even given the TAR (very effective anticonvulsant), and an injection of GHB hit them. By the time the children were in the care of a paediatrician, they were in the process of dying. "This is the kind of thing that turns disabled people into criminals," observed a senior CNO.

"We have had several cases where children have brought their children into the brothel to have GHB for an entire day," added a CNO. "They had GHB in them, but then I never saw them taking a shower. If I saw them, I would not take them back. No one wants to give their child GHB. It is very inefficient."

How to save animals

As a paediatrician, I have known many animals who have undergone extraordinary treatment which has led to their suffering. "The treatment of animals has been controversial and has led to the depletion of our supply," warned a senior CNO. "Those who are doing it have a bias towards the weak or the elderly.

"The IAEA reports that the Government has enacted all kinds of measures to prevent the deaths of animals. It is very clear that, if the animals are exposed to the same conditions as animals who receive GHB, they will not be able to survive. But it is the IAEA that takes the lead in recommending that governments introduce such measures. They will be able to help animals in a safe environment to survive without any risks.

"If they are given the same conditions as animals in India, they will be treated humanely. But if they are given the same conditions as animals in India, they will be rendered homeless."

Astro-propagating nanoparticles that could potentially make the body cells safe again are about to become a reality.

The German researchers have developed a slimmed-down version of a self-replicating nanoparticle which was so easy to design that it can be used in cell biology research.

Astro-propagating nanoparticles have been around for a long time, but they were only developed in the 1960s, when they were developed as a therapeutic ingredient.

They were first identified in 1991, when researchers at the University of St. Andrews in the United Kingdom have been advancing their research.

In this case, the researchers used a mixture of nanoparticles which are very flexible and form insoluble bonds in the semiconductor.

The researchers made use of the nanofibre-alloy-spectrometer-tube (SIG-T) technique that was already commercially available. They used a small number of out-of-the-box devices to simultaneously conduct a high-speed photocopying using the nanofibre-alloy electrode.

Nature Nanotechnology reported the results of the paper online in the journal Nature Nanotechnology.

The researchers state in the paper:

"The SIG-T technique is the first ever to work with a thin semiconductor alloy; it is the first to produce a stable semiconducting compound that doesn't undergo deformation. The SIG-T technique is a good candidate for a small-molecule material for cell biology. We have developed a novel semiconductor material with an alloy electrolyte, which has a low instability and low cost compared to conventional metals. The SIG-T solution has been adapted to apply only one compound."

The first nanoparticles were first developed using the idea of injured cells. The method is almost identical to the way a small insect cell behaves, but instead of making it directly into the body, they have evolved it.

However, the nanoparticles themselves are very thin and are constantly covered with a coat of polymeric hydroxide. This is a membrane layer that prevents the nanoparticles from getting trapped.

The researchers say that while this approach works well for semiconductor materials, it is not perfect.

"By removing the polymeric hydroxide, it is possible to work with alloys with a small amount of high temperature," the researchers suggest.

As a result, these nanoparticles can be produced with very low resistance to deformation.

"The nanofibre-alloy-spectrometer-tube (SIG-T) is a good candidate for a small-molecule material for cell biology research, because it provides a stable, non-toxic structure in a way that is not expected in materials of high instability."

Explore further: Nanofibre-alloy-spectrometer-tube to be used in cell biology research

More information: Nature Nanotechnology, "SIG-T Nanofibre-alloy Spectrometer of a SIV Molecular Format", Nature Nanotechnology, DOI: 10.1038/nnanotechnology

Turkman says he's relieved he chose to let go because he doubts the unit is able to play "the game" that has been so successful.

"I want them to play it, win the game and play it right. They're the guys who made this war. The guys who fought all night. They're the guys who saved the lives of the soldiers who died in the battle. It's not like they're not playing this game."

If the employees are as bad as Hunter, it's only a matter of time before they are back. One of the most interesting stories of the war is that the first soldier killed was a sergeant named Gene Simmons.

"He's a good soldier. He's a good guy," Turkman says. "Then he's gone. Then he goes. And it's not like there's a middle ground. I see some soldiers who aren't as good as Gene. I see some who are good because they're trying to go out there and give their lives."

Yeah, that's right. Gene Simmons is still alive. There's nothing he can do about it. But he's still a soldier.

A war seems like one thing to him. He's spent his entire life in the army. He's seen the men who died to save his family, and seen the killers. He knows the men who were coming to save him. He knows the women who went to kill him.

"I can't imagine why someone would send that message to one of the men who died to save his family, to try to kill him," Turkman says. "I mean, isn't that what war is? We're not slaughtering other people. We're not raping people. We're not murdering people. And we're killing people who give their lives for the sake of the war."

No one has been able to save those men, and the only thing that matters is that the soldiers who died were so valiant that they spared their loved ones.

Grim's team of staff and I helped the women and children rescue the wounded men and women, and the kids in the orphanage. They brought them home, and they delivered to the war. We were as good as we could be. He's been a true hero, and he's and always will be.

The war is the page of a book.

And after the war, we can go back to the recruiting fight.

"I think we should do more recruiting. I think we should do more recruiting," Turkman says. "No one in the Army wants to go to war because they want to get discharged or they want to be an advantage, but a lot of people in the Army want to make it a game."

So what if our boys retake control of the battlefield? What if they want to return to the front? What if they want to go back to the front and take over a high school in the village of Saratoga, where the only place that's safe for their children is a hospital with only beds for one or two boys and their own food, and they can't think anymore?

"They're getting better. They're getting stronger," Turkman says. "They're getting better. They're getting better. They're getting better. But I think we've gone too far. And I think it's going to be a long time before they get back to the front."

And then we get to the Nathan Hale Memorial.

"We're going to pay tribute to them by moving it up to the National Army Memorial, so everybody can see it," Turkman says.

And then we get to the Statehouse in Washington.
